threats.title: V5 ETH Fallback Sends Funds to Aggregator Target Instead of Intended Recipient
threats.hypothesis_description: In _transferOutAndCallV5 ETH path, when the aggregator's swapOutV5 call fails, the fallback sends ETH to aggregationPayload.target (the aggregator contract) instead of aggregationPayload.recipient (the intended user). This differs from V4 transferOutAndCall which correctly sends to the user. If the aggregator lacks a recovery mechanism or its owner is unresponsive, user ETH is permanently lost.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a demonstrable code defect where the wrong recipient address is used in the fallback path. The V4 version uses 'to' (recipient) but V5 uses 'target' (aggregator). Any swap failure results in misdirected funds.

proof_of_signal.pattern: payable(aggregationPayload.target).send(msg.value) sends ETH to aggregator target instead of aggregationPayload.recipient on swap failure

preconditions: Vault calls _transferOutAndCallV5 with ETH (fromAsset == address(0))
Aggregator swapOutV5 reverts or returns false
Aggregator target contract can receive ETH (does not revert)

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Any aggregator swap failure triggers misdirection - natural market conditions (slippage, liquidity) cause this
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: User loses full ETH amount; aggregator contract accumulates stuck ETH

economic_questions: How frequently do aggregator swapOutV5 calls fail in production?
What is the average outbound ETH value per transferOutAndCallV5?
Do whitelisted aggregators have recovery mechanisms for stuck ETH?
validation_requirements: Compare V4 transferOutAndCall fallback (sends to 'to') vs V5 (sends to 'target')
Test with a failing aggregator to confirm ETH goes to target not recipient
Audit whitelisted aggregator contracts for ETH recovery functions

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Full outbound ETH value per failed swap
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: N/A - this is fund misdirection, not attacker profit
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Every failed V5 ETH aggregation swap

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: None - triggered by normal swap failures
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: High-volatility periods increase aggregator swap failure rate

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Any user whose outbound V5 ETH swap through aggregator fails
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All V5 ETH outbound transfers through aggregators

threats.title: V5 ERC20 Aggregation Has No Fallback - Tokens Permanently Stuck on Swap Failure
threats.hypothesis_description: In _transferOutAndCallV5 ERC20 path, tokens are transferred to the aggregator before calling swapOutV5. If swapOutV5 fails, _dexAggSuccess is ignored (by design for batch compatibility), the transaction succeeds, and ERC20 tokens remain stuck in the aggregator with no recovery path from the router. Unlike the ETH path (which has fallback sends) and V4 (ETH-only), ERC20 has no fallback mechanism.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code explicitly ignores the return value of the aggregator call for ERC20s. Combined with the token transfer happening BEFORE the call, this creates a one-way value flow with no recovery on failure.

proof_of_signal.pattern: ERC20 transferred to target, then swapOutV5 called but _dexAggSuccess is unchecked; no fallback to return tokens

preconditions: Vault calls _transferOutAndCallV5 with ERC20 (fromAsset != address(0))
Vault has sufficient _vaultAllowance
Aggregator swapOutV5 fails (slippage, liquidity, revert)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: _dexAggSuccess return value is captured but never checked
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Natural swap failures or malicious aggregator intentionally failing after receiving tokens
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: If aggregator has rescueFunds with malicious owner, they can steal failed swap tokens

economic_questions: What is the volume of ERC20 outbound swaps through aggregators?
Do aggregator contracts have mechanisms to return stuck tokens to users?
Can an attacker deploy a malicious aggregator that intentionally fails to capture tokens?
validation_requirements: Test ERC20 _transferOutAndCallV5 with a failing aggregator
Verify tokens are stuck in aggregator after failure
Check if THORChain nodes can retry failed outbounds

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: 0 (natural swap failures) or aggregator deployment cost for malicious scenario
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Full ERC20 amount per failed swap
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: 100% of stuck tokens if aggregator owner extracts via rescueFunds

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: None for natural failures; DEX liquidity issues increase failure rate
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Market volatility increases swap failure probability

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Users with ERC20 outbound swaps that fail at aggregator
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All ERC20 V5 outbound aggregation transfers

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm no token recovery mechanism exists in router code

threats.title: Permissionless swapOutV5 Allows Draining ERC20 Tokens Stuck in Aggregator
threats.hypothesis_description: THORChain_Aggregator.swapOutV5 is public with no access control. When called with fromAsset != address(0), it approves the swapRouter to spend fromAmount of fromAsset from the aggregator's own balance, then calls swapExactTokensForETH. Any ERC20 tokens stuck in the aggregator (from failed V5 ERC20 swaps per ECON-H002, or any other source) can be drained by anyone calling swapOutV5 with the stuck token as fromAsset and themselves as recipient.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function uses low-level .call to swapRouter, making the aggregator the msg.sender from swapRouter's perspective. Combined with the self-approval, this allows any caller to trigger swaps using the aggregator's token balance.

proof_of_signal.pattern: No access control; safeApprove(fromAsset, swapRouter, fromAmount) approves aggregator's own tokens; low-level .call makes aggregator the msg.sender for swapRouter

preconditions: Aggregator holds ERC20 tokens (from failed swaps, direct transfers, etc.)
DEX pool exists for the stuck token paired with WETH
Attacker calls swapOutV5 with fromAsset=stuckToken, fromAmount=aggregator's balance, recipient=attacker

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: safeApprove(fromAsset, address(swapRouter), fromAmount) uses caller-provided fromAmount against aggregator's balance
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Direct on-chain call to swapOutV5 with attacker-controlled parameters
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker captures all stuck ERC20 tokens, swapped to ETH and sent to their address

economic_questions: How often do V5 ERC20 aggregator swaps fail, leaving tokens in the aggregator?
What is the typical token balance sitting in aggregator contracts?
Can the owner's rescueFunds race to recover before an attacker drains?
validation_requirements: Deploy aggregator, send tokens to it, call swapOutV5 from different address
Verify tokens are swapped and proceeds sent to attacker's recipient address
Check if rescueFunds has priority over swapOutV5 in practice

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Full value of stuck ERC20 tokens in aggregator
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: >99% (gas cost is negligible vs stolen tokens)

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: DEX pool must exist for the stuck token
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Immediately after a failed V5 ERC20 aggregator swap
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Depends on token amount and pool depth

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Users whose tokens are stuck in aggregator from failed swaps
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All ERC20 tokens held by aggregator at any time

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: swapOutV5 access control: none. fromAmount: caller-controlled.
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm swapRouter pulls from aggregator balance via low-level call context

threats.title: Aggregator swapIn Uses Full Contract ETH Balance Including Pre-existing Funds
threats.hypothesis_description: In THORChain_Aggregator.swapIn and AvaxAggregator.swapIn, after swapping tokens to ETH/AVAX via the DEX, the code sets _safeAmount = address(this).balance and deposits the ENTIRE contract balance to the THORChain router. This includes any pre-existing ETH/AVAX in the aggregator from previous operations, direct sends, or selfdestruct. An attacker can call swapIn with minimal tokens to capture all pre-existing ETH under their memo.
threats.investigation_rationale: The use of address(this).balance rather than tracking the specific ETH received from the DEX swap creates a balance attribution vulnerability. Any ETH sitting in the aggregator is credited to the next swapIn caller.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _safeAmount = address(this).balance captures entire contract balance, not just swap output

preconditions: Aggregator has pre-existing ETH/AVAX balance (from failed swaps, direct sends, or selfdestruct)
Attacker has some tokens with a DEX pair to execute a valid swap
DEX swap succeeds for at least a minimal amount

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: _safeAmount = address(this).balance (line 89) instead of tracking swap output delta
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Call swapIn with dust tokens; deposit captures entire aggregator ETH balance
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker captures pre-existing_ETH + swap_output under their THORChain memo

economic_questions: How much ETH/AVAX typically accumulates in aggregator contracts?
Can ETH accumulate from ECON-H001 (failed V5 fallback sends to aggregator)?
What is the cost of executing a minimal swap to trigger the balance capture?
validation_requirements: Send ETH to aggregator, then call swapIn from different address
Verify the deposit amount includes the pre-sent ETH
Measure balance-before vs balance-after delta compared to address(this).balance

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Minimal tokens for DEX swap + gas
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: ~0.01-0.05 ETH in gas and swap fees
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: All pre-existing ETH in aggregator (0 to unbounded)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Depends on accumulated balance; potentially very high

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Any DEX pair for the attacker's token
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After a period of failed aggregator swaps or direct ETH sends

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Protocol/users whose ETH was stuck in aggregator
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All ETH/AVAX balance in aggregator contracts

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Compare pre-swap and post-swap balances to verify attribution error

threats.title: batchTransferOutAndCallV5 Reuses msg.value for Every ETH Aggregation Call
threats.hypothesis_description: In batchTransferOutAndCallV5, each _transferOutAndCallV5 call for ETH (fromAsset == address(0)) uses msg.value as the ETH value sent to the aggregator. If a batch contains N ETH-based entries, the first sends all msg.value, and subsequent entries attempt to send msg.value again. Since the router's ETH balance is depleted after the first call, subsequent calls fail through the fallback chain until .transfer() reverts the entire batch. If the router has any pre-existing ETH balance, it could be drained by the second entry before reverting.
threats.investigation_rationale: msg.value is a transaction-level constant that doesn't decrease as calls are made. The code uses msg.value directly instead of tracking remaining ETH balance or using per-item amounts.

proof_of_signal.pattern: aggregationPayload.target.call{value: msg.value} used in loop - msg.value is reused for every ETH entry

preconditions: Batch contains 2+ entries with fromAsset == address(0)
THORChain nodes construct such a batch (operational assumption)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: msg.value sent N times in loop, but only 1x msg.value available
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Batch with multiple ETH entries causes full batch revert
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: DoS on batch outbound processing; potential pre-existing ETH drain if router holds any

economic_questions: Do THORChain nodes ever construct batches with multiple ETH aggregation entries?
Can the batch revert cause downstream issues like stuck outbound queues?
Could the router accumulate ETH that gets drained before revert?
validation_requirements: Construct a batch with 2 ETH entries, verify revert
Test if any pre-existing ETH is consumed before revert
Check node batch construction logic

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: N/A - DoS vector, not direct profit

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Any time batch is constructed with multiple ETH entries

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All users in the affected batch
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All ETH in the failed batch

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: msg.value constant vs loop iteration count
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Deploy and test with multi-ETH-entry batch

threats.title: batchTransferOutV5 ETH Accounting Mismatch Between msg.value and Payload Amounts
threats.hypothesis_description: In batchTransferOutV5, each _transferOutV5 for ETH uses transferOutPayload.amount (not msg.value) for the .send() call. There is no on-chain validation that msg.value equals the sum of all ETH payload amounts. If msg.value > sum(ETH amounts), excess ETH is permanently locked in the router. If msg.value < sum(ETH amounts), the router may draw from pre-existing ETH balance or cause cascading reverts via .transfer() fallback.
threats.investigation_rationale: The single-item transferOut uses msg.value as safeAmount (correct). The batch version uses payload.amount per item with no aggregate validation. This asymmetry creates an ETH accounting gap.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _transferOutV5 uses transferOutPayload.amount for send, not msg.value; no sum validation in batch function

preconditions: Vault calls batchTransferOutV5 with msg.value != sum of ETH payload amounts
Or router has pre-existing ETH balance from previous operations

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Excess msg.value permanently locked; deficit uses pre-existing balance
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: ETH leakage into router with no recovery mechanism

economic_questions: How often do THORChain nodes construct batches with mismatched ETH amounts?
Is there any mechanism to recover ETH locked in the router?
Can pre-existing router ETH be drained via crafted batch?
validation_requirements: Test batchTransferOutV5 with msg.value > sum(amounts) - verify ETH locked
Test with msg.value < sum(amounts) - verify fallback behavior
Check if router can accumulate ETH balance over time from this

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: N/A - operational risk rather than attacker exploit

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Cumulative excess ETH across all batches

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: msg.value vs sum of ETH payload amounts in batch
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Compare single transferOut (uses msg.value) vs batch (uses payload.amount)

threats.title: safeApprove in _routerDeposit Incompatible with USDT-Style Approve, Blocking Router Migration
threats.hypothesis_description: In _routerDeposit (used by transferAllowance and returnVaultAssets for cross-router migration), safeApprove(asset, router, amount) sets approval directly without first resetting to 0. USDT's approve() requires current allowance to be 0 before setting a non-zero value. If any stale allowance exists to the new router, the approve call will revert, permanently blocking migration of USDT from the old router to the new one.
threats.investigation_rationale: The protocol documentation states it expects to handle 'weird' ERC20 behaviors including USDT. The safeApprove pattern does not reset to 0 first, which is a known incompatibility with USDT's approve implementation.
deployment_assumptions: Router is used to custody USDT or tokens with USDT-style approve race protection
threats.config_evidence: USDT.sol provided in codebase; protocol docs state support for any ERC20 including those with non-standard behaviors

proof_of_signal.pattern: safeApprove calls approve(address,uint256) without first calling approve(address,0); USDT requires allowance=0 before setting non-zero

preconditions: USDT or similar token is custodied in the router
Router migration triggered (churn to new router)
Any stale allowance exists from a previous partial migration attempt

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Natural migration failure or griefing by creating stale approvals
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: USDT permanently locked in old router if migration cannot complete

economic_questions: What volume of USDT is custodied in THORChain routers?
How frequently do router migrations occur (vault churn)?
Can a griefing attack create stale approvals to block migration?
validation_requirements: Test _routerDeposit with USDT when stale allowance exists
Verify approve reverts and migration is blocked
Check if approve(0) + approve(amount) pattern would fix this

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Dust amount for griefing (creating stale approval)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: All USDT locked in old router (indirect)

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All USDT depositors with funds in the old router
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Full USDT balance locked until workaround
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All USDT custodied by the router

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: USDT approve behavior vs safeApprove implementation
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Test with USDT approve race condition

threats.title: AvaxRouter deposit() Is Public, Bypassing Expiry Protection
threats.hypothesis_description: AvaxRouter.deposit() is public and callable directly, bypassing the block.timestamp < expiration check enforced by depositWithExpiry(). In contrast, THORChain_Router's _deposit() is private and only accessible via depositWithExpiry(). This means AvaxRouter deposits can execute at any time regardless of the user's intended expiration, potentially leading to unfavorable swaps during stale/delayed transactions.
threats.investigation_rationale: The inconsistency between THORChain_Router (private _deposit) and AvaxRouter (public deposit) suggests an oversight. The protocol documentation states deposits should be rejected after expiration.
threats.config_evidence: deploy.ts deploys AvaxRouter without any wrapper; deposit() is directly callable

proof_of_signal.pattern: deposit() is public payable with no expiration check, unlike THORChain_Router where _deposit is private

preconditions: User or integration calls deposit() directly instead of depositWithExpiry()
Transaction is included in a block after the user's intended deadline

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Stale transactions execute at unfavorable rates
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: User receives worse swap rate than intended

economic_questions: Do any integrations or frontends call deposit() directly on AvaxRouter?
What is the typical delay between user intent and on-chain inclusion on Avalanche?
How much slippage could occur during delayed execution?
validation_requirements: Verify deposit() is directly callable without expiration
Compare access patterns between THORChain_Router and AvaxRouter
Check if Bifrost or other infrastructure uses deposit() directly

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: High-volatility periods increase stale execution risk

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Users calling deposit() directly during congestion
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Slippage between intent time and execution time
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All deposits routed through public deposit()

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm no expiry check in AvaxRouter.deposit

threats.title: Aggregator swapIn Over-Approves swapRouter for Fee-on-Transfer Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: In THORChain_Aggregator.swapIn, safeApprove(token, swapRouter, amount) uses the original caller-specified amount, not the actual received _safeAmount (after fee-on-transfer deduction). This leaves a residual approval on the swapRouter. While the DEX swap uses _safeAmount correctly, the stale over-approval could be exploited in certain scenarios, and creates an accounting inconsistency.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code correctly measures actual received tokens via balance delta (_safeAmount) but then approves the original amount. For fee-on-transfer tokens, amount > _safeAmount, creating a dangling approval.
deployment_assumptions: Fee-on-transfer tokens are used with the aggregator's swapIn function
threats.config_evidence: Protocol docs state support for fee-on-transfer tokens. USDT.sol in codebase has fee mechanism.

proof_of_signal.pattern: safeApprove(token, address(swapRouter), amount) uses original amount, not _safeAmount from balance delta

preconditions: Fee-on-transfer token used in swapIn
Token's fee causes amount > _safeAmount
Stale approval persists for swapRouter

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: safeApprove(token, swapRouter, amount) where amount > _safeAmount for fee tokens
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Residual approval of (amount - _safeAmount) left on swapRouter
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Stale approval could be exploited if aggregator receives more of the same token later

economic_questions: What fee-on-transfer tokens are commonly used with THORChain?
Can the stale approval be exploited in a follow-up transaction?
Does the swapRouter have any mechanism to use stale approvals?
validation_requirements: Test swapIn with a fee-on-transfer token
Verify residual approval amount after swap
Assess exploitability of stale approval

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After a swapIn with a fee-on-transfer token

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Next user of same token through aggregator
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Value of fee portion of previous transaction

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Check if approve(amount) should be approve(_safeAmount)

threats.title: ETH_RUNE transferTo Uses tx.origin Enabling Token Theft via Any Intermediary Contract
threats.hypothesis_description: ETH_RUNE.transferTo(recipient, amount) uses tx.origin instead of msg.sender to determine the source of funds. Any contract in the call chain can call transferTo to transfer tokens from the original transaction signer without their approval. A malicious contract could call transferTo(attacker, victim_full_balance) during any interaction with the victim, draining all their ETH_RUNE.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code explicitly uses tx.origin and even documents the risk in comments. While this is a known design choice for upgrade compatibility, it creates a severe vulnerability that could be exploited by any contract the user interacts with.
threats.config_evidence: Protocol docs state ETH_RUNE is deprecated and out of scope. However, the contract is in the auditable list and the code defect is real.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _transfer(tx.origin, recipient, amount) - uses tx.origin instead of msg.sender

preconditions: Victim holds ETH_RUNE tokens
Victim interacts with any contract (even unrelated to RUNE)
That contract calls ETH_RUNE.transferTo(attacker, amount)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: _transfer(tx.origin, recipient, amount) bypasses approval system entirely
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Deploy malicious contract; victim interacts with it for any reason; drain all RUNE
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker captures victim's entire ETH_RUNE balance

economic_questions: How many users still hold ETH_RUNE?
What is the total value of ETH_RUNE in circulation?
Has ownership been renounced as documented?
validation_requirements: Deploy a contract that calls transferTo in its fallback/function
Have a test user interact with it while holding ETH_RUNE
Verify full balance is transferred to attacker

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Gas for deploying malicious contract (~0.01 ETH)

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: DEX liquidity to sell stolen RUNE
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Could depress RUNE price if large amounts sold

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Any ETH_RUNE holder who interacts with a malicious contract

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm transferTo bypasses all approval checks
